title: South Asia Snapshot - July 6, 2026
text:
South Asia Snapshot - July 6, 2026

Trade, Diplomacy, and a New Calculus of South Asia’s Foreign Policy

Dear Readers,June felt like one of those months when almost everyone was on the move. Leaders met, trade negotiators got back around the table, and some semblance of peace resumed. From U.S.-Iran talks and the Modi-Trump meeting to Tarique Rahman's visits to Malaysia and China, June wasn't short on headlines. The bigger question, though, was what they revealed about where countries in the region think the world is heading and how they are positioning themselves for it. Let's dive in.India Balances U.S. Ties Amid Wider OutreachThe G7 Summit in France last month gave President Trump and Prime Minister Modi their first face-to-face meeting in over a year amid strained bilateral ties. It also came at a sensitive moment. Just days earlier, U.S. strikes on commercial vessels in the Gulf of Oman killed three Indian seafarers, prompting further diplomatic discomfort. While the meeting delivered the classic praise from Trump for Modi, it stopped short of offering much reassurance that the relationship is back on solid ground. Beyond Trump's optimism on trade talks, there was little in the way of tangible outcomes. Trade remained front and center. U.S. Trade Representative Jamieson Greer arrived in New Delhi soon after, with both sides signaling that an interim agreement is within reach. But "close" has become a familiar word in these negotiations. India continues to push for preferential tariff treatment over regional competitors as both sides strive to reach a balanced deal.New Delhi is also not waiting around, and that was evident from Modi’s wider middle power engagement on the G7 sidelines. While talks with Washington continue, the UK-India FTA comes into force this month, negotiations with the EU are picking up pace ahead of a targeted year-end conclusion, and India and Canada have recommitted to finalizing a Comprehensive Economic Partnership Agreement by the end of 2026 as well. Whether by design or necessity, India is spreading its bets.As the BRICS Chair this year, India also hosted the BRICS National Security Advisers' meeting last month, though much of the attention fell on the sidelines meeting between indian NSA Ajit Doval and China's Foreign Minister Wang Yi as both sides continue constructive and forward-looking engagement.Another notable visit was that of Myanmar's Min Aung Hlaing to New Delhi. The discussions covered border management, defense, trade, technology, and rare earths cooperation. The optics may be controversial internationally, but from New Delhi's perspective, the logic is straightforward. Geography, border security, and economic imperatives make engagement difficult to avoid.Why It Matters: While it is true that the United States remains one of India's most important strategic partners, New Delhi’s confidence in Washington's long-term reliability has weakened. An interim trade deal would certainly provide some relief, although it remains unclear when it will materialize. More importantly, India no longer appears willing to leave too much riding on one relationship.That caution is being shaped by more than trade. Washington's renewed engagement with Pakistan, mixed signals on China, a quieter Quad, and the U.S. Department of War's recent announcement to revert the Indo-Pacific Command to its original Pacific Command designation have all prompted questions in New Delhi about the long-term consistency of U.S. strategic priorities. The same uncertainty is emerging in technology. While India continues to deepen cooperation with the United States on critical minerals and emerging technologies, including through initiatives such as Pax Silica, it is also confronting the reality that access to cutting-edge U.S. technologies is increasingly shaped by export controls and national security considerations. Restricting access to Anthropic’s latest AI models for foreign nationals serves as another reminder that technological partnerships are becoming as geopolitical as they are commercial.Together, these pressures are nudging India to hedge in pursuit of its economic and strategic interests, even if pragmatic engagement with Washington continues.Islamabad: Diplomacy and DividendsAfter weeks of mediation, diplomacy finally delivered a breakthrough. On June 17, the United States and Iran agreed to a 14-point interim agreement, extending the ceasefire by 60 days, including in Lebanon, reopening the Strait of Hormuz, and lifting the U.S. naval blockade. Known as the Islamabad MoU, the agreement was digitally signed by the United States and Iran, with Pakistan signing in its role as the main mediator.The MoU offers a framework to buy both sides time to negotiate a more durable settlement. As talks between the United States and Iran unfolded in Switzerland soon after, in the presence of Pakistani and Qatari officials, it became evident that the road to peace would need sustained commitment from the principal parties.Why It Matters: For years, Islamabad has struggled to project itself as more than a country managing overlapping security and economic crises. Its recent role in bringing Washington and Tehran to the negotiation table offers a different narrative, one of a country that is capable of facilitating outcomes rather than simply reacting to them. The praise from Gulf partners, President Trump, and Iranian President Masoud Pezeshkian, whose first post-conflict visit was to Pakistan last month, reflects that growing diplomatic credibility.The challenge now is ensuring that this diplomatic goodwill does not become a fleeting moment. A more stable U.S.-Iran relationship could create practical opportunities for Pakistan, from access to more affordable Iranian energy to renewed discussion around the long-stalled Iran-Pakistan gas pipeline if sanctions continue to ease. Equally, warmer ties with Washington need to evolve beyond security cooperation into greater investment opportunities and critical minerals cooperation. Pakistan has earned diplomatic capital; the next test is whether it can convert that into economic gains that outlast the headlines.Bangladesh FirstTarique Rahman's first overseas visits were notable as much for where he didn't go as where he did. Rather than beginning with either India or China, the two regional powers Bangladesh is often expected to balance, Rahman chose to stop in Malaysia before travelling to China.In Malaysia, discussions focused on reopening labor market access for Bangladeshi workers, attracting investment, and launching negotiations towards a Free Trade Agreement. China offered an equally important economic agenda. Rahman met President Xi Jinping; signed several MoUs covering trade, investment, and infrastructure; and both sides agreed to elevate their comprehensive strategic cooperative partnership to jointly build a 'China-Bangladesh community’ with a shared future in the new era.Why It Matters: Rahman's first foreign visits were less about balancing India against China than about signaling a governing philosophy: Bangladesh First.Economic recovery appears to be the organizing principle of the new administration's foreign policy, and that naturally lends itself to a broader "Look East" approach. This does not mean Dhaka is turning away from New Delhi. Both countries continue to have shared economic, security, and social ties despite recurring political frictions.What is changing is Bangladesh's willingness to diversify. China's support for projects such as the Teesta River Comprehensive Management and Restoration Project, alongside its backing for Bangladesh's engagement with BRICS and the SCO, gives Beijing an expanding role in the country's growth and development agenda. That does not diminish India's importance, but it does raise the bar. If New Delhi wants to remain Bangladesh's partner of first choice, it will need to compete more through delivery on trade, connectivity, and people-to-people ties. The decision to resume tourist visas is a welcome start, but sustaining influence may require a much broader economic proposition.Stay Tuned! We will be watching the region closely as PM Takaichi wraps up an important India visit and rhetoric around the Indus Waters Treaty gathers pace once again. More on this next month!Warmly,Farwa Aamer
